steps.title: Download JDK
steps.body:
To write and run Java programs, you need the **JDK (Java Development Kit)**. The JDK includes **javac** for compiling code and **java** for running programs.

Download steps:
1. Open your browser and go to **Adoptium** (https://adoptium.net)
2. Click the "**Latest LTS Release**" button
3. The Windows installer will be downloaded automatically

| Term | Meaning |
|------|------|
| JDK | Complete toolkit required for Java development |
| LTS | Long-Term Support version (stable and reliable) |
| JRE | Runtime-only environment (included in JDK) |
steps.tip: Choose JDK version **17** or **21**. These are LTS (Long-Term Support) versions, so they will be stable and supported for a long time.

steps.title: Run the Installer
steps.body:
Double-click the downloaded **.msi file** to run it.

| Screen | Action |
|------|------|
| Welcome screen | Click "Next" |
| Installation location | Click "Next" as-is (no change needed) |
| Custom setup | **Check "Set JAVA_HOME variable"** and click "Next" |
| Installation | Click "Install" |
| Completion | Click "Finish" |

**Checking "Set JAVA_HOME variable" is the most important step.** This automatically configures the necessary environment variables for you.
steps.tip: Using the Adoptium installer automatically sets environment variables (JAVA_HOME and PATH), saving you the trouble of manual configuration.

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.body:
Let's verify that the JDK was installed correctly.

**How to open Command Prompt:**
1. Press the **Windows key** on your keyboard
2. Type "**cmd**"
3. Click **"Command Prompt"** when it appears

Once the black screen opens, type the following commands one line at a time and press Enter.
steps.tip: If you see "'java' is not recognized as an internal or external command...", **restart your computer** and try again. If that doesn't work, manually add the JDK's **bin folder** (e.g., C:\Program Files\Eclipse Adoptium\jdk-17\bin) to the PATH environment variable.

steps.title: Set Up an Editor (VS Code)
steps.body:
Prepare a **text editor** for writing programs. **Visual Studio Code (VS Code)** is recommended for beginners.

**VS Code installation steps:**
1. Go to https://code.visualstudio.com in your browser
2. Click the "**Download for Windows**" button
3. Run the downloaded installer
4. Follow the on-screen instructions and click "Next"

**Adding Java extensions:**
1. Launch VS Code
2. Click the square icon (**Extensions**) on the left side
3. Type "**Extension Pack for Java**" in the search box
4. Click "**Install**" for the displayed extension

| Feature | What it does |
|------|----------|
| Code completion | Shows suggestions as you type |
| Error display | Highlights mistakes with red underlines |
| Debugging | Run your program step by step |
steps.tip: You can compile and run Java with Notepad too, but using VS Code significantly improves efficiency with **syntax highlighting** and **error display**.

steps.title: Run Your First Program
steps.body:
Let's create and run an actual Java program!

**File creation steps:**
1. Create a new folder called "**JavaTest**" on your Desktop
2. In VS Code, go to "File" → "Open Folder" → select the "JavaTest" folder
3. Click the "New File" icon in the left panel
4. Name the file **HelloWorld.java** (match uppercase/lowercase exactly)
5. Copy and paste the code below, then save (Ctrl + S)

**Compile and run:**
1. In VS Code, open "Terminal" → "New Terminal"
2. Run the following commands in order
steps.tip: The file name must be **exactly the same as the class name** (HelloWorld.java). Uppercase and lowercase must match precisely. If you see "Hello, World!" displayed, your environment setup is **complete**!

steps.title: Open Terminal
steps.body:
On Mac, you'll use the **Terminal** app to install the JDK.

**How to open Terminal:**
1. Press **Command + Space** on your keyboard (opens Spotlight search)
2. Type "**Terminal**"
3. Click "Terminal.app" to open it

Terminal is a tool for giving commands to your computer by typing text. It might feel a bit difficult at first, but all you need to do is **copy & paste commands and press Enter**.
steps.tip: Adding Terminal to the "Dock" is convenient for quick access in the future. Right-click the Terminal icon → "Options" → "Keep in Dock".

steps.title: Install Homebrew
steps.body:
**Homebrew** is a tool (package manager) for easily installing software on Mac. Using Homebrew is the easiest way to install the JDK.

To check if Homebrew is already installed, run the following in Terminal. If a version number is displayed, it's already installed.
steps.code:
# Check if Homebrew is installed
brew --version

# If not installed, copy & paste the following and run
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
steps.tip: During the Homebrew installation, you'll be asked for a password. Enter your **Mac login password** (nothing will appear on screen as you type, but this is normal).

steps.title: Install JDK
steps.body: Use Homebrew to install the **JDK (Java Development Kit)**. Run the following commands in Terminal.
steps.code:
# Install JDK 17
brew install openjdk@17

# Create a link so the system recognizes the JDK
sudo ln -sfn $(brew --prefix)/opt/openjdk@17/libexec/openjdk.jdk /Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/openjdk-17.jdk
steps.tip: You'll be asked for a password when running the **sudo** command. Enter your Mac login password. If you prefer not to use Homebrew, you can also download a .pkg installer from **Adoptium** (https://adoptium.net) and run it (no link creation needed in that case).

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.body:
Let's verify that the JDK was installed correctly. Run the following commands in Terminal.

If version information (e.g., openjdk 17.0.x) is displayed, the installation was successful.
steps.tip:
If the version is not displayed, try **closing and reopening Terminal**. If that doesn't work, add the following to your shell configuration file (~/.zshrc):
export JAVA_HOME=$(/usr/libexec/java_home)

steps.title: Set Up an Editor (VS Code)
steps.body:
Prepare a **text editor** for writing programs. **Visual Studio Code (VS Code)** is recommended.

**VS Code installation steps:**
1. Go to https://code.visualstudio.com in your browser
2. Click the "**Download for Mac**" button
3. Unzip the downloaded .zip file and move it to the Applications folder

**Adding Java extensions:**
1. Launch VS Code
2. Click the square icon (**Extensions**) on the left side
3. Search for and install "**Extension Pack for Java**"

| Feature | What it does |
|------|----------|
| Code completion | Shows suggestions as you type |
| Error display | Highlights mistakes with red underlines |
| Debugging | Run your program step by step |
steps.tip: The default TextEdit app on macOS uses "Rich Text" mode by default. If you use it, make sure to switch to "Format" → "Make Plain Text" in the menu.

steps.title: Run Your First Program
steps.body:
Let's create and run an actual Java program!

**File creation steps:**
1. Create a new folder called "**JavaTest**" on your Desktop
2. In VS Code, go to "File" → "Open Folder" → select the "JavaTest" folder
3. Click the "New File" icon in the left panel
4. Name the file **HelloWorld.java**
5. Copy and paste the code below, then save (Command + S)

**Compile and run:**
1. In VS Code, open "Terminal" → "New Terminal"
2. Run the following commands in order
steps.tip: The file name must be **exactly the same as the class name** (HelloWorld.java). If you see "Hello, World!" displayed, your environment setup is **complete**!

steps.title: What Is MySQL
steps.body:
**MySQL** is a **database management system** for storing and managing data. The technology for connecting to MySQL from a Java program to read and write data is called **JDBC**.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|------|
| MySQL | A free database system |
| JDBC | A mechanism for accessing databases from Java |
| SQL | A language for issuing commands to databases |
| Table | A unit for storing data in tabular format |

This guide covers everything from installing MySQL to creating a practice database.
steps.tip: Database topics are covered in Chapter 10 and beyond in this app. It's fine to learn the basics of Java first before starting this section.

steps.title: Install MySQL
steps.body:
Install according to your OS.

**[Windows]**
1. Go to https://dev.mysql.com/downloads/installer/
2. Download "**MySQL Installer for Windows**"
3. Run the installer and select "**Server only**"
4. Follow the on-screen instructions and set a **root password**

**[Mac]**
Use Homebrew to install with the following commands.
steps.code:
# Mac: Install and start MySQL
brew install mysql
brew services start mysql

# Set root password
mysql_secure_installation
steps.tip: Make sure to note down the **root password** you set during installation. You'll need this password every time you connect to the database.

steps.title: Verify MySQL Is Running
steps.body:
Let's verify that MySQL is running correctly. Run the following in Command Prompt (Windows) or Terminal (Mac).

When prompted for a password, enter the **root password** you set during installation.
steps.tip:
If you see "mysql: command not found", MySQL's bin folder is not added to PATH.

**Windows:** Add C:\Program Files\MySQL\MySQL Server 8.0\bin to PATH
**Mac:** Check if mysql is "started" with brew services list

steps.title: Prepare the JDBC Driver
steps.body:
To connect to MySQL from Java, you need the **JDBC driver** (mysql-connector-j).

**Download steps:**
1. Go to https://dev.mysql.com/downloads/connector/j/
2. Select "**Platform Independent**"
3. Download the **.zip** file and extract it
4. Copy the **mysql-connector-j-x.x.x.jar** file to your project folder

You need to specify this .jar file path when compiling and running.
steps.code:
# Specify JDBC driver when compiling (Mac/Linux)
javac -cp .:mysql-connector-j-8.x.x.jar MyApp.java
java -cp .:mysql-connector-j-8.x.x.jar MyApp

# For Windows, use ; as the separator
javac -cp .;mysql-connector-j-8.x.x.jar MyApp.java
java -cp .;mysql-connector-j-8.x.x.jar MyApp
steps.tip: **-cp** is an option to specify the classpath (where Java looks for libraries). **.** (dot) means the current folder. On Windows, the separator is **;** (semicolon), and on Mac/Linux it's **:** (colon).

steps.title: Create the Practice Database
steps.body:
Let's create the database and table for JDBC practice. Log in to MySQL and execute the following SQL statements in order.

Table structure to create:

| Column | Type | Description |
|----------|------|------|
| id | INT (Primary Key) | Employee ID (auto-increment) |
| name | VARCHAR(50) | Employee name |
| department | VARCHAR(30) | Department |
| salary | INT | Monthly salary |
steps.tip: This **employees** table is used in JDBC practice exercises. Each SQL command is covered in detail in Chapter 10 "Database Introduction" of this app.

steps.title: What Is Spring Boot
steps.body:
**Spring Boot** is a **framework** (a development foundation tool) for easily building web applications in Java.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|------|
| Spring Boot | Web development framework for Java |
| Maven | Tool for library management and builds |
| Endpoint | A URL for accessing the web app |
| localhost | Your own computer (for development) |

This guide covers creating a Spring Boot project and verifying it works.
steps.tip: Spring Boot is covered in Chapter 38 of this app. We recommend learning Java basics, object-oriented programming, and exception handling first.

steps.title: Create a Spring Boot Project
steps.body:
Use the **Spring Initializr** web tool to auto-generate a project template.

**Steps:**
1. Go to https://start.spring.io in your browser
2. Configure the settings as follows

| Setting | Value |
|----------|----------|
| Project | **Maven** |
| Language | **Java** |
| Spring Boot | **Latest stable version** (without SNAPSHOT) |
| Group | com.example |
| Artifact | demo |
| Packaging | Jar |
| Java | 17 |

3. Click "**ADD DEPENDENCIES**" on the right and add "**Spring Web**"
4. Click "**GENERATE**" to download the ZIP
5. Extract the ZIP and save it to your preferred location
steps.tip: If using VS Code, adding both the "**Extension Pack for Java**" and "**Spring Boot Extension Pack**" extensions is recommended.

steps.title: Verify Project Startup
steps.body: Let's verify the project works correctly. In Command Prompt (Windows) or Terminal (Mac), navigate to the extracted folder and run the following command.
steps.code:
# For Windows
.\mvnw spring-boot:run

# For Mac / Linux
./mvnw spring-boot:run
steps.tip: On successful startup, you'll see the message "**Started DemoApplication**". Open your browser and go to **http://localhost:8080**. If you see a "**Whitelabel Error Page**", the app is running correctly (the error is normal since we haven't created any pages yet). Press **Ctrl + C** to stop the app.

steps.title: Create a Hello World Endpoint
steps.body:
Let's create a page you can access from a browser. Create the following file.

| Item | Content |
|------|------|
| File name | HelloController.java |
| Location | src/main/java/com/example/demo/ |
| Role | Defines the action when a URL is accessed |
steps.tip: After saving the file, restart the app and go to **http://localhost:8080/hello** in your browser. If you see "**Hello, Spring Boot!**", your web app development environment setup is **complete**!

steps.title: What Is Eclipse
steps.body:
**Eclipse** is one of the most widely used **Integrated Development Environments (IDEs)** for Java development. You can write, run, and debug code all in one screen.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|------|
| IDE | A tool that integrates code editing, execution, and debugging |
| Eclipse | A standard IDE for Java development (free) |
| Pleiades | A package that adds Japanese localization and useful plugins to Eclipse |
| Tomcat | A web server for running Servlets / JSP |
| Spring Tool Suite | A plugin for Spring Boot development in Eclipse |

With Eclipse, you can complete everything from project creation to execution **using only the GUI**, compared to VS Code + command line.
steps.tip: This guide uses **Pleiades All in One**. It includes Eclipse, Japanese localization, JDK, and Tomcat all together, making it easy for beginners to set up.

steps.title: Install Eclipse (Pleiades)
steps.body:
**Steps:**
1. Go to https://willbrains.jp in your browser
2. Click "**Eclipse 2024**" (latest yearly version)
3. Select your OS (**Windows** / **Mac**)
4. Download the **Java** "**Full Edition**"

| Edition | Contents |
|-------------|------|
| Standard | Eclipse + plugins only |
| Full Edition | Eclipse + **JDK** + **Tomcat** bundled (recommended) |

**Windows:**
- Extract the downloaded zip to a short path like **C:\pleiades** (avoid the Desktop)
- After extraction, double-click **eclipse.exe** to launch

**Mac:**
- Open the downloaded dmg and drag **Eclipse** to the **Applications** folder
- On first launch, if you see "Cannot verify the developer", go to **System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway**
steps.tip: With the Full Edition, you don't need the JDK installation from the Windows/Mac setup guides. However, if you want to use javac/java commands from the command line, you'll need to install JDK and configure PATH separately.

steps.title: Create a Servlet / JSP Project
steps.body:
**Servlet** and **JSP** are fundamental technologies for creating web applications in Java. Eclipse and Tomcat make development easy.

**Steps:**
1. Launch Eclipse and select **File → New → Dynamic Web Project** from the menu
2. Enter "HelloServlet" as the **Project name**
3. Select **Apache Tomcat** as the **Target runtime** (if not listed, specify installed Tomcat via "New Runtime")
4. Click "**Finish**"

| Term | Meaning |
|------|------|
| Servlet | A Java program that handles HTTP requests |
| JSP | A mechanism for embedding Java code in HTML |
| Dynamic Web Project | A project format for Servlets / JSP |
| Tomcat | A web server for running Servlets / JSP |
steps.tip: If you're using the Full Edition, Tomcat is already included so no additional installation is needed.

steps.title: Run Your First Servlet
steps.body:
Let's add a Servlet to the project and display it in a browser.

**Steps:**
1. Right-click **src/main/java** in the project → **New → Servlet**
2. Enter "com.example" as the **Package name** and "HelloServlet" as the **Class name**, then click "**Finish**"
3. Modify the **doGet** method in the generated code as shown below
4. Right-click the project → **Run As → Run on Server** → select **Tomcat** and click "**Finish**"
steps.tip: Go to **http://localhost:8080/HelloServlet/HelloServlet** in your browser. If you see "**Hello, Servlet!**", it's successful. To stop Tomcat, click the stop button in the "**Servers**" view in Eclipse.

steps.title: Create a Spring Boot Project in Eclipse
steps.body:
By adding the **Spring Tool Suite (STS) plugin** to Eclipse, you can also create and run Spring Boot projects within Eclipse.

**STS plugin installation:**
1. Go to **Help → Eclipse Marketplace** from the Eclipse menu
2. Type "**Spring Tools**" in the search box
3. Click "**Install**" for "**Spring Tools 4**" and follow the instructions
4. **Restart** Eclipse

**Creating a Spring Boot project:**
1. Select **File → New → Spring Starter Project** from the menu
2. Configure as follows

| Setting | Value |
|----------|----|
| Name | demo |
| Type | Maven |
| Packaging | Jar |
| Java Version | 17 |
| Group | com.example |

3. Click "**Next**" and check **Spring Web**
4. Click "**Finish**" to auto-generate the project
steps.tip: This allows you to do the same thing as the **Spring Initializr** (website) introduced in the "Web App Development Environment Setup" guide, but within the Eclipse interface.

Let's add a controller to the Spring Boot project from Step 5 and run it.

**Steps:**

2. Enter "HelloController" as the **Class name** and click "**Finish**"
3. Paste the code below into the generated file
